data.title: Friedrich Vorwerk Group: A Hidden Champion Powering Germany's Energy Transition
data.description:
The energy transition in Europe and especially in Germany is in progress. However, it brings a number of challenges with it, which we have already looked at in my previous article. One of the challenges is the need to expand the energy infrastructure.

With the German energy transition and the planned massive expansion of electricity, gas and hydrogen grids, the focus is shifting to a medium-sized company that only a few investors are familiar with: Friedrich Vorwerk Group SE (OTCPK:FDVWF). While many investors are looking at major players such as Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMEGF) or E.ON (OTCPK:EONGY), Vorwerk is working in the background to implement precisely those infrastructure projects that are essential for the success of the transformation. The key question is therefore: will this previously rather unknown company become the hidden champion of a major economic trend?

What Does Friedrich Vorwerk Actually Do?

Friedrich Vorwerk is a company specializing in energy infrastructure solutions. The group offers engineering and planning services as well as the physical construction of pipelines, power cables and technical facilities and their ongoing maintenance. The portfolio also includes complete turnkey projects, such as power lines, hydrogen grids and gas compressor stations. With 19 subsidiaries and around 1,950 employees at 15 locations, the company covers the entire value chain and is mainly operating on the German market.

With its business model, the Friedrich Vorwerk Group covers all central areas of energy infrastructure and generates its revenue from four distinct segments. The largest segment, accounting for 37% of total revenue, is the Electricity segment, in which the company builds underground power lines, substations and high-voltage facilities - particularly in relation to the expansion of renewable energies. Around 34.5% of sales are generated by the Natural Gas segment, which comprises the construction and connection of gas pipelines, some of which are already intended for later conversion to hydrogen. The still young but strategically important hydrogen segment accounts for around 5.3% of total sales. In addition, the Adjacent Opportunities segment - which includes turnkey solutions for district heating and biogas - accounts for around 23.2% of sales. This shows that Friedrich Vorwerk not only has a broad technological base, but is also well diversified in terms of its sources of revenue. This broad-based structure of capabilities makes the company a sought-after partner for the expansion and conversion of the energy supply in Germany. In this context, its most important customers include all major German transmission system operators.

The shareholder structure of the Friedrich Vorwerk Group consists of two anchor investors and the free float. The investment company MBB SE holds the majority of the shares with over 50 percent, while CEO Torben Kleinfeldt owns around 18 percent. The remaining almost 30 percent is in free float. Both MBB SE and the CEO are longtime shareholders and therefore interested in long-term shareholder value. In addition, the CEO has a background in the industry and has been with the company since 2001. This constellation ensures a long-term strategic orientation and strong ties between the anchor investors and the company.

Solid Growth Outlook Backed by Infrastructure Expansion

In the coming years, Germany is facing a huge transformation in its energy infrastructure, which is being actively driven forward by government investment programs. The expansion of the infrastructure is essential in order to meet the projected electricity demand of plus 149 percent by 2045 and at the same time make the transition to renewable energies. Around EUR 300 billion are to be invested in laying new power cables and the associated infrastructure. At the same time, the development of a hydrogen economy is being vigorously pursued: The hydrogen core network is to grow to over 9,000 kilometers by 2032 - supported by around EUR 19 billion in investment. A further EUR 4.6 billion in state subsidies and EUR 3.4 billion in private capital will flow into hydrogen projects. The recent political decision to set up an EUR 500 billion infrastructure fund, parts of which will be invested in energy infrastructure, is intended to underpin the German government's plans for expansion. This means that future market growth is not only technically necessary, but also politically desirable, and the financing is secured.

In this environment, the Friedrich Vorwerk Group is following a clear growth path based on both organic expansion through the acquisition of new infrastructure projects and through targeted acquisitions of smaller companies. For example, Seyde und Coburg GmbH was acquired in April 2024 in order to further strengthen expertise in the field of cathodic corrosion protection. These acquisitions are highly strategic and strengthen the company's overall portfolio.

A look at the past shows that Vorwerks growth of around 20% in the years 2019 - 2023 was quite solid. At the same time, 2022 and 2023 were characterized by supply difficulties and associated project delays, which had a negative impact on growth and profitability. After two weaker years, sales growth of 33% to EUR 498.4 million was achieved in 2024. Furthermore, the order backlog increased to around EUR 1.2 billion (EUR 1 billion in 2023) - a clear indication of the solid underlying capacity utilization in the coming years. Particularly dynamic growth was seen in the Electricity division, where revenue more than doubled thanks to projects such as the A-Nord line.

For 2025, a further increase in sales of up to 8.5-14 % is expected - with margins remaining stable. This growth will also be maintained in the following years due to numerous upcoming project tenders and a solid existing order backlog. The operational turnaround after difficult years with supply shortages and project delays is clear evidence of this: Friedrich Vorwerk is back on a sustainable growth path, supported by political decisions and technological necessity.

What Are Vorwerk's Competitive Advantages?

Friedrich Vorwerk operates in a highly competitive market, in which large companies such as Bilfinger SE and Spie SA often take the lead in major projects due to their financial strength and global economies of scale. These companies can benefit from lower prices and greater capacity in large infrastructure projects, which poses challenges for Vorwerk as a medium-sized company. Vorwerk also competes with specialized companies such as Siemens Energy AG, which already offers established solutions, in the field of power grid construction.

Despite this competition, Vorwerk has decisive competitive advantages. Particularly noteworthy is the company's comprehensive expertise, which offers everything from planning to construction and maintenance from a single source. This represents a clear advantage over smaller competitors, who often do not have the resources to implement larger projects. These smaller providers therefore usually concentrate on smaller infrastructure projects at distribution network level, whereas Vorwerk is also able to implement large and complex infrastructure projects. Vorwerk is also able to offer integrated solutions from a single source, which represents a clear advantage for customers.

Vorwerk also scores points for its flexibility and specialization. The company can react quickly to new market requirements, particularly in the field of hydrogen and pipeline construction, where many large companies are less active. This gives Vorwerk a clear competitive advantage in niche areas. Its strong market position in Germany and long-standing relationships with grid operators such as OGE, TenneT and Gasunie also help to secure regional projects.

One of Vorwerk's unique selling points is its many years of experience in the construction and conversion of gas, hydrogen and electricity lines. While many competitors are only just beginning to enter the hydrogen market, Vorwerk already has a deep understanding of the conversion of existing infrastructure.

Another important advantage of Vorwerk is its economic efficiency. The operating margin and free cash flow margin show that the company achieves a higher profitability compared to the peer group. In the period from 2020 to 2024, Vorwerk achieved a higher operating margin than many competitors in most years, with the exception of the difficult years 2022 and 2023:

These key figures illustrate that, despite occasional fluctuations in margins, Vorwerk operates very cost effectively compared to the competition and manages to achieve higher profitability in most years.

Overall, it is not only its many years of expertise and comprehensive range of services that are convincing, but also its ability to operate economically and flexibly. The combination of experience in the construction and conversion of infrastructure, a clear focus on niche markets and a strong market position in Germany enables the company to successfully assert itself in a highly competitive market.

Vorwerk’s Profitability: How Strong Are the Numbers?

The profitability of Friedrich Vorwerk is above average compared to the industry as a whole and underlines the economic efficiency of the company. We have already seen from key figures such as the operating margin and the free cash flow margin that Vorwerk achieves a higher profitability than many of its competitors.

The project business is often dependent on individual large orders and their timing and financial variables, which can lead to significant fluctuations in margins. In this context, both the return on equity and the return on assets are important key figures for assessing the long-term profitability of the company. Here, too, it is clear that Vorwerk has an advantage over its peer group. Despite the fluctuations in the project business, Vorwerk has been able to maintain stable profitability in recent years, which is strong in comparison to the industry as a whole and indicates solid corporate management and efficient capital utilization.

Overall, it is clear that Vorwerk is able to achieve above-average profitability despite the fluctuations in the project business. This reflects the clear competitive advantages and shows that the company not only remains competitive in its sector, but is also in a position to grow profitably.

How Stable Is Vorwerk's Financial Foundation?

Friedrich Vorwerk is highly financially stable, providing the company with a solid basis for further growth and successful project implementation. Particularly noteworthy is an equity ratio (EKQ) of 46.9%, which is well above the industry average and an indicator of a strong capital structure.

Cash and cash equivalents of EUR 174.6 million provide the company with financial flexibility. This not only enables a flexible response to market developments, but also a high level of liquidity for financing current projects as well as possible acquisitions and expansions. In combination with financial liabilities of only EUR 22.4 million, net debt with regard to interest-bearing liabilities is EUR -152.2 million. Overall, it can be stated that Vorwerk has a very solid and long-term financing model. The low level of debt, the high equity ratio and the high level of cash and cash equivalents enable the company to remain stable even in turbulent times and offer scope for further growth.

What Could Go Wrong?

Friedrich Vorwerk is exposed to various risks that could have a considerable impact on the company in the coming years. One important risk is political dependency, as Vorwerk is heavily dependent on government regulations and government investment.

In particular, the planned infrastructure projects such as SuedLink could be affected by political changes. The German government is currently reviewing whether the demand for electricity and the expansion of transmission grids are really necessary to the extent planned. This could potentially result in a delay to the projects or a reduction in the scope of the project.

There is also a discussion about the laying of transmission grids: If legal regulations for underground lines are lifted, the proportion of above-ground lines could increase significantly, which would change the cost calculation for projects such as SuedLink and OstLink - a disadvantage for Vorwerk, which currently relies on underground installation.

Another risk relates to the planned expansion of the hydrogen grid, the scope of which is currently being questioned. If this is reduced, this could impair Vorwerk's future growth in the area of hydrogen infrastructure.

In addition, infrastructure projects are generally characterized by long planning and construction times as well as bureaucratic hurdles. These uncertainties and delays, which frequently occur with major projects such as the SuedLink or the expansion of the hydrogen grid, make long-term planning reliability more difficult and impair profitability.

Overall, Vorwerk's business model is heavily dependent on how the political framework conditions develop and how crucial hydrogen and gas will remain for the energy supply of the future.

Although all of these potential risks seem threatening at first, a few aspects should be taken into account:

  1. Due to Vorwerk's size and specialization, even a reduction in infrastructure expansion would still provide a large number of growth opportunities. If, for example, the electricity transmission grids were to run 50% above ground as a result of legislative changes, the investment volume for expansion would fall from EUR 300 billion to EUR 192 billion. This may seem a lot at first, but it is not a major problem for a company with a total annual revenue of around EUR 500 million, as there are still sufficient opportunities for growth.
  2. As part of my research, I spoke to several experts from the energy sector. They confirmed to me that, from a purely technical point of view, an expansion of the gas and electricity infrastructure is necessary in order to guarantee a secure supply in the coming years. From a purely technical perspective, it is therefore very unlikely that the current reviews will result in a significant reversal of the current targets and requirements.

Valuation: Justified or Overstretched?

In order to make an appropriate valuation, here is a quick overview of the current framework and conditions: A look at the current situation reveals that the difficulties of the last two years, which have resulted from project delays and higher costs due to delivery difficulties, should have been successfully resolved. Revenue in 2024 increased by 33% compared to the previous year. At the same time, an EBIT margin of 15.8% was achieved, which represents an increase of 262% compared to the previous year. This increase will not be able to be continued on this scale, as the initial situation was relatively weak. However, it is clear that growth and margins are returning to normal compared to the weak years. Based on these figures, the solid order backlog and the outstanding growth prospects for the energy infrastructure sector, we can also expect strong growth in the coming years. In my view, this outlook should also be reflected in the valuation assumptions.

I used the discounted cash flow (DCF) model to value Vorwerk. I made conservative assumptions for the calculation in order to make a realistic estimate and to build a certain margin of safety into my model.

I assumed growth rates of 14% for the first three years, 12% for the following three years and 10% for the subsequent years. A perpetuity growth rate of 2% was used for the terminal value. At first glance, these growth rates appear optimistic, but in view of the level of planned investment in infrastructure expansion, they should be classified as conservative.

The FCF margin is assumed to be between 3.7% and 5% - this is in line with the industry average and Vorwerk's average figures over the last five years.

In addition, the calculation was based on data from Aswath Damodaran. The cost of equity was set at 8.3% and the cost of debt at 6.7%, resulting in a WACC of 7.5%.

These assumptions result in a fair value of around EUR 43. In view of the current market price of EUR 57, the company is therefore around 32% overvalued.

Key Takeaways and What They Mean for Investors

As a specialist for energy infrastructure in the electricity and gas sector, Friedrich Vorwerk is a key player in the restructuring of the German energy supply. The company is very well positioned to benefit from the upcoming expansion of the grids and offers solid growth prospects for the coming years.

With a clear focus on its niche, high operational efficiency and practically no debt, Vorwerk is one of the qualitatively convincing companies in the infrastructure segment. The strong profitability, coupled with an experienced management and long-term orientation, underlines the stability and quality of the business model.

Although there are political risks - such as possible adjustments to the grid expansion or uncertainties regarding hydrogen - the fundamental need for modern infrastructure remains technically undisputed and politically supported.

From a valuation perspective, however, the company's current share price is well above its fair value of around €43. Accordingly, an entry at the current price level does not appear attractive. For long-term investors who focus on quality companies at a reasonable price, I recommend keeping an eye on the company and considering an entry at a more favorable valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Eventbrite: Missteps Will Lead This Company Lower
data.description:
What will a weak global macroeconomy mean for an already-struggling business? I encourage investors to continue to deploy careful stock picking in order to not find out firsthand. Already, we've seen many indicators that consumer demand is waning, ranging from slowdowns at retailers to airline carriers announcing a plan to cut capacity for routes that fail to fill up seats.

Against this backdrop, the backdrop for consumer spending on experiences and live events will certainly suffer, and Eventbrite (NYSE:EB) was already on a thin tightrope with little wiggle room for error. The online ticketing company has already lost ~40% of its value since the start of the year. But amid a decay in its event creator base and paid ticket volumes, I think the losses have much deeper to run.

I last wrote a bearish article on Eventbrite in December, when the stock was still trading in the mid $3s. Since then, shares of Eventbrite have fallen deeper than the broader market, as revenue has declined double digits and creators increasingly move away from the problem.

Nobody likes a middleman, especially in a tough economy where everybody from individual households to the federal government is trying to cut spending. And with this in mind, why would event creators pay a fee to Eventbrite? Presumably, it's to gain access to Eventbrite's customer base and achieve better marketing and distribution for their events. But amid recent missteps, Eventbrite's entire ecosystem is shrinking, including both paid creators and event goers.

In Q4, faced with mounting backlash over its decision to implement organizer fees even on free events, Eventbrite decided to get rid of these fees in Q4. The company notes that this move has driven a resurgence in free event creators and free event tickets (but, of course, not revenue) - but the loss of this fee income will represent a $20 million revenue headwind in FY25 - impacting 6 points of y/y growth.

The company is clearly trying to re-generate goodwill for its ticketing ecosystem, but its core metrics show persistent decline. The number of paid tickets and paid event creators are declining. Consumers have a smorgasbord of options, ranging from direct rivals like Ticketmaster to direct purchases from event organizers. Other players in the travel ecosystem, like Booking.com, alongside smaller players like Klook and Viator that focus solely on experiences, are also competing to sell consumers tickets to live events. And in the wake of a tough macroeconomy with less disposable consumer spending, it's a shrinking pie that Eventbrite will have to fight tooth and nail for.

All in all, now is not the right time to catch the falling knife on Eventbrite. With so much uncertainty in the horizon and negative trends pervading, Eventbrite hasn't found the bottom yet.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Eventbrite's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Eventbrite's revenue declined -13% y/y to $76.5 million in the quarter, decelerating sharply relative to a -5% decay in Q3, owing to the loss of marketplace revenue from free event organizers fees.

The good news is that these actions have re-stimulated activity in the free events space. As shown in the chart below, tickets for free events grew 8% y/y in Q4 (versus a huge -17% decline in Q3), helping bump total tickets growth to 2% y/y. Interesting to observe is the fact that even though paid tickets remained in decline, trends improved to a -10% decline, four points healthier than -14% in Q3. No doubt, Eventbrite is trying to generate a halo effect for its revenue generating paid events by trying to earn back more goodwill in its paid events, hoping that more creators and event-goers on the platform as a whole will help to stimulate growth.

Paid creators also improved slightly sequentially versus Q3, which has previously been in decline for several quarters. The company added roughly ~3k paid creators, with 166.4k total in Q4. Increasingly, however, Eventbrite is moving away from trying to host as many creators and events as possible: it's focusing its sales efforts on the largest repeat event organizers, as these are the creators that drive the most ticketing volume and are more willing to spend on promoted ads. On top of a specialized sales team to manage relationships with these creators, the company is giving these creators access to more advanced tools, such as audience segmentation and social media marketing tools alongside data and analytics.

The company notes that improving ticket trends improved into early Q1. Through January, paid ticketing trends have improved for five consecutive quarters. Per CEO Julia Hartz's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Total ticketing volume returned to growth, up 2% year-over-year in Q4. As we had anticipated, free ticket volume recovered faster, growing 8% in Q4, which was a 25% point swing from Q3, and paid ticketing trends improved sequentially from Q3 to Q4.

Additionally, paid transacting creators and paid event volume improved. That momentum has continued into 2025. Looking at January's year-over-year performance, both paid ticket sales and paid transacting creators improved over the previous month and ticket sales per creator also continued to strengthen. We're now tracking five consecutive months of improving year-over-year paid ticket trends, adjusting for last year's leap year impact."

But the core question is: to what degree can this be sustained? Eventbrite assumes that it will return to paid ticketing growth by the second half of FY25. But of course, at the time of Eventbrite's Q4 earnings print, the stock market hadn't yet taken a nosedive and reciprocal tariffs weren't on the daily headlines. With consumer spending tracking downward, can Eventbrite really sustain this momentum?

We note as well that an increasing portion of Eventbrite's revenue growth has come from ads, driven by creators who pay Eventbrite to more broadly feature their events on the platform. All manner of advertisements typically fall during a recession.

The elimination of high-margin free event organizer fees, whose ultimate impact on improving paid tickets is still unknown, will also have a material impact on Eventbrite's profitability that offset the company headcount eliminations. As shown in the chart below, Eventbrite's adjusted EBITDA margins in Q4 declined one point y/y to 9%.

Management is only expecting a "mid-single digit" adjusted EBITDA margin for FY25, which I interpret to be between a 4-7% margin: materially lower than 11% in FY24, which benefited from the high-margin organizer fees, and still lower than 9% in FY22-FY23 (in which only the tail end of FY23 includes the fees). The message here: structurally, Eventbrite's difficulty in achieving scale and paid ticket growth is making the business structurally less profitable, even after multiple rounds of job cuts.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices just north of $2, Eventbrite trades at a very small market cap of $210.1 million. After we net off the $230.3 million of cash and restricted cash (netting off funds owed to creators) against $240.7 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet, Eventbrite's resulting enterprise value is $220.5 million.

For FY25, the company is guiding to $295-$310 million of revenue, representing a decline range of -9% to -5%, with $20 million (6 points) of impact from the elimination of organizer fees. We should be prepared for estimates to come down lower, as I expect the company will report on weaker ticket trends in the wake of growing macro uncertainty in Q1.

If we interpret the company's mid-single digit adjusted EBITDA guidance to mean 6% (-5 points y/y) against the midpoint of the company's revenue range, adjusted EBITDA in FY25 would be $18.2 million (-48% y/y). This puts Eventbrite's valuation at 12.1x EV/FY25 revenue.

For a company in decline, and at risk of downward guidance revisions, Eventbrite isn't even terribly cheap. Its net debt position and risk of sliding from a profit to losses if ticket trends continue to erode makes it a very risky buy.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: AppLovin Tiptoes Beyond Its Niche And Into A Trillion Dollar Opportunity
data.description:
I initiated coverage on AppLovin Corp (NASDAQ:APP) back in November last year, where I discussed the disruptive potential inherent to the company. AppLovin has been under the spotlight since the AI proliferation boom began dominating market sentiment in around 2024.

While the market had awoken to the paradigm-shifting nature inherent to APP in November last year, around the time I had initiated coverage on the stock, there have been some significant changes in the company's outlook. As I will elaborate in this update, I believe there are clear indications that AppLovin is increasingly positioned for hypergrowth, and towards dominating the digital ad ecosystem in an AI-defined tech context.

The company's expansion beyond its historical gaming niche, the rapidly expanding digital ad market, and AppLovin's efforts towards automation and self-service all substantially boost its value proposition. Considering how cheap the stock continues to trade, I re-iterate my buy stance on the stock.

Expansion Beyond Gaming

The company's recent results, and in particular its Q4 performance marked an inflection point, with the company, for the first time ever, marking its ground into non-gaming categories such as ecommerce. This, effectively, validated the idea that AppLovin's platform creates value beyond the direct-to-consumer brand category. In particular, the company's management, while not disclosing specific figures for individual verticals, reported that APP captured substantial ad spend from the holiday shopping season.

Without getting too technical about the company's tech systems, this impressive performance in new verticals indicate that AppLovin's fundamental technology and underlying AI-based algorithms possess a far broader applicability than had previously been known. It essentially opens up a massive opportunity for the company, and positions it to lead the digital ad ecosystem.

In its recent earnings call, in February, AppLovin's management highlighted that there are 10 million businesses worldwide engaged in online advertising, so the company, despite its improving capabilities, still has the overwhelming bulk of the market share yet to tap into. In fact, BCC Research estimates the digital ad market to climb to $1.2 trillion by as early as 2027, citing key growth drivers such as the proliferation of high-speed internet, extensive adoption of smartphones, etc. APP with its total revenue of $4.7 billion has only locked into a fraction of this rapidly expanding market. It's stepping outside of the gaming niche is certainly a move in the right direction.

In addition to its growth prospects, I also continue to be impressed by the company's incremental demand generation. To keep it simple, users on AppLovin's network (which consists of over 1 billion daily active users in mobile games, with an engagement level rivalling that of social media platforms), aren't simply redirecting existing purchases upon being exposed to digital ads, but are discovering and spending on new product categories altogether. AppLovin places strong emphasis on incrementality, and in particular its objective of driving incremental revenue growth for advertisers on its network. This, of course, refers to the additional value created for advertisers through AppLovin's platforms, than would have come about through purely organic campaigns. I believe AppLovin's focus on incrementality-related metrics sets it apart from any other player looking to lead the digital ad space because it not only objectively demonstrates the effectiveness of its ad platform, but also builds trust based on objective metrics and evidence. The value proposition, in my opinion, is unparalleled.

Strong Growth in the Numbers

It is because of the aforementioned dynamics, I believe APP was able to pull off such impressive numbers in its financial results. The company's topline growth for 2024 amounted to 44%. In terms of operating income, in 2023, AppLovin set a new record, reporting its highest ever figure of $650 million. For 2024, the company nearly tripled this earlier record, and ended up with an operating profit total of $1.9 billion.

Things on the bottom-line are just as impressive, with net income more than quadrupling from $356 million in 2023, to about $1.6 billion in 2024.

Performance on the margin side also blows me away, with gross profit margin expanding from 67.74% to 75.22%, operating income margin surging from 19.78% to 39.8%, and net income margin more than tripling from 10.87% to 33.55%. What is remarkable is this all took place in a single year, within which the company has grown rapidly. Normally, when a company's scale up to this degree, the first metrics to take a hit are its margins. This is clearly not the case with APP. Its efficient scaling, through the use of automated AI tools, indicates how it is avoiding a bloated operational model.

The management affirmed in its earnings call that in order to cater to additional demand growth, it would need to increase focus on investing in self-service capabilities. Moving forward, this would be a key priority for the company, and I believe it would ensure that margins can continue to expand as APP achieves even greater scale. Through self-service tools, powered by AI, advertisers would be able to onboard and manage campaigns, while accessing performance data without extensive manual intervention from AppLovin's team.

Combined with the rapidly growing digital ad ecosystem, this would position AppLovin to unlock the full potential of the market opportunity at its disposal and lead this fast-evolving space.

Valuation Assessment

In my November analysis on AppLovin, I determined that APP was in the buy zone, owing to its EV/Sales ratio of 18.6, and its forward looking topline growth rate of over 24%.

While the stock's EV/Sales ratio hasn't changed in the last 5 months, its forward revenue growth has actually climbed to more than 28%. This improved growth outlook on the topline, coupled with its unchanged EV/Sales ratio, is a positive sign that the value opportunity in APP has actually improved, since my initial coverage.

While my valuation assessment priorly hinged on topline figures, I think the company's EBITDA figures, could also be quite useful. I personally like EBITDA, as a measure, quite a lot because it is more useful both than a company's revenue, and its net income. It makes for a far more fair comparison with peers as it eliminates accounting adjustments, and it is more useful in terms of determining how much value a company is creating for its shareholders.

Based on the EBITDA metric, APP actually is proven to be a faster growing outperformer of the IT sector, than in the case of its revenue:

Seeing this significant outperformance (which is even more impressive on the trailing figures than on the forward-looking ones), I felt that the value opportunity here is at least worth investigating. In this spirit, I've plotted APP against 83 peers from the Application Software industry, and the results can be seen in the scatter graph below:

As we can see, APP, based on its positioning above the trendline, is clearly situated in the buy zone, as we have previously determined. It offers a forward EBITDA growth rate of almost 50%, at an EV/EBITDA of 38. For comparison, the $55 billion visualization software company, Autodesk (ADSK), is also trading at an EV/EBITDA of around 38, but only offers EBITDA growth of less than 13%.

Overall, APP is very attractive compared to its peers in the industry. Although its EV/EBITDA ratio may seem like the stock is expensive at a surface level , its high growth more than justifies its pricing. Given the value it offers, I strongly feel that the stock is actually pretty cheap.

For these reasons, I reiterate my buy rating on APP.

Risk to Thesis

I believe AppLovin faces two major risks that could substantially limit the growth potential it currently faces. First, at this stage of the company's growth trajectory, execution risks are highly critical. The company is facing increased demand, a lot of which is coming from outside its historical, gaming-related niche. Coupled with its increasing shift to incorporating self-service features into its platform, AppLovin would need to strive to ensure it does not compromise on the elements that make up its competitive moat, such as its user experience, for example.

Secondly, I believe that competition from entrenched incumbents such as Meta, Google, and Amazon should not be underestimated. Each of these tech giants are spending aggressively on new technology (a lot of which may be AI-based) to ensure they retain their market share, and not lose it to (relatively) new entrants, such as AppLovin.

Investor Takeaway

While APP has been in growth mode for several months now, owing to its disruptive positioning in the rapidly growing digital ad ecosystem, its recent moves beyond the direct-to-consumer model enormously expand its total growth potential. The company has only touched the surface of the online advertising market, and is poised to dominate through its AI-powered platform. I am strongly of the belief that the future is AppLovin.

data.title: Nvidia Warns On China License Issue
data.description:
One of the best growth stories in recent years has been Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA). The chipmaker has seen its revenues explode as companies look to take advantage of the Artificial Intelligence ("AI") boom. On Tuesday, however, the company detailed some bad news in the after-hours session, reporting that it would take a large charge due to a needed license for chip exports to China. As a result, shares took a meaningful hit, and questions will now increase regarding the short term growth picture.

Previous coverage of the name:

I last covered the chip giant back in early March, right after the company reported its fiscal fourth quarter results. While the revenue beat detailed was the smallest in this recent revenue upswing, current quarter guidance looked a bit more impressive. The company has become a profit and cash flow monster recently, and revenue estimates were continuing to soar.

Recently, US markets have been quite volatile over trade war and economic concerns, with President Trump's tariff plans seemingly changing by the day. Since I last covered Nvidia, the S&P 500 is down almost 8% (through Tuesday's close), but the chipmaker had held up quite a bit better, down less than 2%. That changed quite a bit on Tuesday afternoon.

Tuesday's unfortunate news:

Over the past couple of years, the US government has placed various restrictions on AI chip exports to China, fearing the potential for a growing technology rival. Nvidia designed a chip, known as H20, that was not as advanced, and thus would comply with export regulations. There was a report last week that the Trump administration was considering the pause of potential export restrictions on the H20. However, on Tuesday afternoon, we received an 8-K filing from Nvidia that detailed the following:

On April 9, 2025, the U.S. government, or USG, informed NVIDIA Corporation, or the Company, that the USG requires a license for export to China (including Hong Kong and Macau) and D:5 countries, or to companies headquartered or with an ultimate parent therein, of the Company’s H20 integrated circuits and any other circuits achieving the H20’s memory bandwidth, interconnect bandwidth, or combination thereof. The USG indicated that the license requirement addresses the risk that the covered products may be used in, or diverted to, a supercomputer in China. On April 14, 2025, the USG informed the Company that the license requirement will be in effect for the indefinite future.

As a result of this, Nvidia expects to record up to approximately $5.5 billion of charges associated with H20 products for inventory, purchase commitments, and related reserves. These charges will be taken in the company's fiscal first quarter, which ends on April 27th. We'll get quarterly results from Nvidia in mid to late May, and while GAAP results will definitely take a hit, I'm sure analysts will be more focused on the adjusted results that come. Investors will also be curious to see if guidance shows the additional $4.5 billion of expected sequential growth that the street is currently looking for.

Analyst estimates level off:

Questions will now be asked about Nvidia's ongoing business in China. Recently, there were reports of a surge in Nvidia chip orders from Chinese firms that were looking to get ahead of a potential export ban. In recent quarters with its newest chips, Nvidia's biggest issue has been supply, so we don't yet know if the company was able to fill some or all of these orders. The report above also said that the extended lead time to fill all of this potential demand could easily last beyond any export ban date.

With growing concerns over a trade war and potential US recession, Nvidia finds itself in a situation we haven't seen in the past couple of years. As the chart below shows, the analyst revenue estimate average for the current January 2026 is not increasing at a decent clip anymore. In fact, since I last covered the name, the number has actually decreased by about $160 million.

At the current estimate, Nvidia is still forecast to grow its top line by more than 56%. That's quite impressive for a company expected to do over $200 billion in total sales at this point. According to CNBC, the H20 generated between $12 billion and $15 billion in revenue last year for Nvidia, so if those sales stop, the company's growth rate will drop a bit.

A look at current valuations:

Going into Tuesday's close, Nvidia shares went for a little less than 25 times their January 2026 expected earnings. Competitor Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) went for about 20.5 times its calendar 2025 expectation, while Intel Corporation (INTC) is at more than 41 times, thanks to almost no adjusted profitability. Nvidia is projected to show the most revenue and earnings growth among the three (when not counting Intel's swing back to adjusted profitability), despite working off some pretty tough base numbers after last year's dramatic growth for the top and bottom lines.

As for street analysts, the picture is exceptionally bullish. Of the 62 that had a rating on the stock in the past three months, 45 had strong buys and 11 had buys, with the rest being holds. The average price target currently stands at nearly $168, implying almost 50% upside from Tuesday's close. I should note, however, that the average has come down a little more than $5 since my previous article on the name.

Final thoughts and recommendation:

Nvidia investors received some bad news on Tuesday when the company announced a large one-time charge related to its H20 chip sales to China. The chipmaker states it needs a license for these exports, and thus will record $5.5 billion in extra expenses for the current quarter. As a result, shares dropped about 6% in the after-hours session, trading down to around $105. Perhaps the only good news here is that these lower prices will allow the company to repurchase shares at a cheaper price for its share buyback over time.

While the headlines here certainly are not good, I'm sticking with my buy rating on the stock today. Even if the company loses some China sales this year, it is still increasing overall revenues at a rate that's much faster than its competitors, and the valuation is quite fair for that growth. With the AI boom set to continue for some time, Nvidia is the definite leader in this space and likely the best name to own here as a result.

data.title: Zeroes Across The Board
data.description:
Tuesday granted Wall Street a welcome rest, closing in a sea of zeroes resembling salary negotiations at an unpaid internship fair. Morning gains faded into the ether, but when the worst anyone can muster is a 0.4% loss, we’ll call it a collective shrug in pinstriped pajamas.

Level Change 4/15/25 (%)

-0.4 Dow

-0.1 Nasdaq

+0.2 Nasdaq 100

-0.2 S&P 500

-0.1 S&P 400

-0.2 S&P 600

The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) simmered down to 30 after last week’s tariff shock sent it boiling into the 50s. Solid bank earnings reminded investors that there’s more business going on than headline-writing. Cynics insist we’re seeing more selling of rips than buying of dips, but the S&P’s five-day, 8% climb suggests someone forgot to panic properly.

Trade remains the big question mark enchilada, as markets search for tariff off-ramps from the administration’s opening salvo of shock and awe. At this point, a country could toss in collectible finance minister action figures with every shipment, and economists would call it a gold-standard deal.

So you know it must be cricketville in US-EU talks when the most optimistic headline is “little progress.” Brussels looks down in the sprout, resigning itself to lingering tariffs as Washington’s industrial archaeologists dream of repatriating industry one factory floor at a time.

If Europe’s dour, China’s spitting mad - banning rare-earth exports to the US and Boeing (BA -2.4%) imports to Beijing. Bloomberg says Chinese airlines were told to refuse Boeing jets and stop buying anything aircraft-related from the US, presumably including those tiny bags of pretzels. Luckily for Boeing, only 130 planes in its 5600-strong backlog were headed for China—less than 2%, about what the stock fell today.

American exceptionalism might not be stuffed and mounted in a Beltway gift shop just yet. Yesterday’s appetite for Treasuries carried over, nudging the 10-year yield down to 4.35%, and even the wheezing but upright dollar strengthened for a change.

In a PR blitz, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent assured the public that his department has a “big toolkit” to manage any surge in yields, but swears there’s scant evidence of abandonment in the global shopping mall, no sad little Treasury note waiting on a bench outside H&M.

From our Not Good file: The New York Fed’s April Empire State Manufacturing Index rose to -8.10 in April from March’s -20.00, beating expectations. But it’s still just shrinking less rapidly, a rebound only a statistician could love, and input and selling prices rose for the fourth straight month to their highest in more than two years. And for the first time since 2022, firms turned pessimistic about the future, notching the second-lowest outlook in the survey’s 20-year history.

But the Good file gives a nod to Band-Aid baron Johnson & Johnson (JNJ -0.5%), which grew sales and earnings in Q1 and - grab your chair arms - raised full-year guidance, bumped its dividend, and more or less said, “Tariffs? Never heard of ’em.” Thanks to risk of supply chain shortages, brand name pharma gets a pass. Let the 80% foreign-finished generics do the sweating.

Such were the market murmurings today, when traders napped, Brussels pouted, the Treasury Secretary brandished his big toolkit, and the shampoo-to-chemo empire chuckled at tariffs.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: RVER: Can This New Active GARP ETF Deliver On Its Bold Objective?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article initiates my coverage of the Trenchless Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:RVER), an actively managed fund holding just 12-25 securities selected for their growth-at-a-reasonable-price characteristics. Through its unique approach to GARP investing, River1 Asset Management hopes RVER will outperform the S&P 500 Index over all time horizons, regardless of whether the market is bullish or bearish. I'll assess the likelihood of that happening by evaluating RVER against passive ETF peers, and even though I'm skeptical of the claim, I think it's an interesting fund to watch, so I've assigned it a neutral "hold" rating for now. I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

RVER Overview

Strategy Discussion and Peer Group

According to its website, RVER's growth-at-a-reasonable price approach "eliminates many of the dead or non-growing companies in an index, while strategically avoiding stocks with overpriced valuations or risk to earnings." With a goal of capital appreciation, RVER aims to outperform the S&P 500 Index by selecting 12-25 stocks its managers believe will outperform in both bull and bear markets. RVER's expense ratio is 0.65%, and as of April 14, 2025, the ETF has $70 million in assets under management.

The fund's prospectus document adds that River1 uses bottom-up analysis to select stocks "utilizing a model that focuses on three primary screens," covering the following:

  • Valuation: multiples of free cash flow, net income, EBITDA, and sales.
  • Growth: top and bottom-line sales and net income growth
  • Institutional Ownership: managers review number and size of ownership

The team also looks for outliers that are likely to miss earnings expectations and removes them from the screening process. However, not much else is disclosed, and I was disappointed to see no mention of quality. That gave me the idea of comparing RVER with the iShares MSCI USA Quality GARP ETF (GARP) and the Xtrackers Russell 1000 US QARP ETF (QARP), which take into account quality. In my analysis, I'll also include the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) as the benchmark RVER seeks to outperform, as well as the Invesco S&P 500 GARP ETF (SPGP), which also considers quality by evaluating financial leverage and return on equity.

RVER's 0.65% expense ratio is high but normal for an actively managed fund. Of the factor funds listed, SPGP is the most established, but readers should know it only began tracking its current Index in June 2019, so it's actually QARP that has the longest and most relevant track record, having launched in April 2018.

Performance Snapshot

RVER has a short history, having only launched one year ago on April 2, 2024. I think it's too early to tell if the approach has merit, but RVER is about 3% behind SPY, and its 2.32% total return also trails GARP and QARP. The bright spot is that it's easily outperformed SPGP, which is down 11.00%. I haven't looked into SPGP deeply in a while, but the last time I checked, it was structured more like a value fund than a growth fund, and I wasn't convinced it was a better option than SPY.

RVER Analysis

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between RVER, SPY, SPGP, GARP, and QARP. I caution that this is a mere snapshot, and the fund managers appear to be quite active, evidenced by the 153% turnover rate noted in the fund's annual report. Needless to say, things can change quickly, but I imagine they'll be consistent in emphasizing a particular style.

Right away, we see how RVER is concentrated in Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary, which comprise 52.86% of the portfolio. RVER overweights these sectors by about 15% compared to the peer group average, with the main offsets in Energy and Financials. That's not surprising for a growth fund, as those two sectors are value-oriented, but RVER underweights Technology by about 7% compared to SPY, so that should help with its valuation ratios.

RVER's top ten holdings are listed below and are led by Alphabet (GOOGL), Lyft (LYFT), and On Holding AG (ONON). One observation is that size doesn't seem to play any factor whatsoever in either the selection process or the weighting scheme. RVER has its fair share of mega-cap growth stocks, but 8/19 stocks in its portfolio have market caps below $20 billion. Evidently, managers select from a wide universe of stocks and simply look for the best opportunities from whatever size segment makes sense.

RVER Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for all of RVER's 19 holdings. Most of these are related to growth, value, and quality, but I also highlighted some volatility and momentum statistics, as I'd like to use these to better understand RVER's approach.

Here are four observations to consider:

RVER: Outstanding Growth At A Reasonable Price

My first observation is to confirm that RVER absolutely delivers on its growth-at-a-reasonable-price mandate. Over the last five years, its components have increased sales by an annualized 26.55% compared to 9-17% for its peers. In addition, analysts expect next year's sales and EBITDA to increase by 19.07% and 26.09%, respectively, which is almost twice the rate of SPY. The portfolio trades at 19.30x forward earnings and 15.88x trailing cash flow, which is so close to SPY's ratios that I wonder if it's intentional. The objective could simply be to maximize the portfolio's growth features while matching SPY's value, subject to a few reasonable constraints.

RVER: Volatility Is Excessive

Rational investors don't mind volatility, so long as it's in their favor. That seems to be the mindset of RVER's managers, as the current portfolio has an incredibly high 1.36 weighted average five-year beta. This metric, which is a measure of market risk, means it's much riskier than a broad-based market fund like SPY. In fact, the beta is so high that I decided to run a backtest on the current holdings to understand what could happen if these stocks retreat. After excluding two stocks to extend the length (MBLY, CART), here are the results with monthly rebalancings.

The hypothetical results are great, but that 43.83% drawdown from November 2021 to September 2022 is substantial. These stocks clearly don't do well in market downturns, so I think it's crucial readers ask themselves if they really want to get involved in a high-risk play right now.

RVER Momentum: Managers Appear To Consider Annual Price Position

One statistic you might not be familiar with is a stock's Annual Price Position. To my knowledge, it's not calculated elsewhere, but it's simple to calculate using the following formula:

[Current Price - 52W Low Price] / [52W High Price - 52W Low Price]

The result tells you where a company's stock price is currently trading in relation to its 52-week high and low price, with higher values indicating strong momentum factor exposure. After making this calculation for all 19 stocks, I was fascinated that every single one had a value below 50%, indicating they're closer to their 52-week low prices. This could be how River1 seeks out good value stocks, and since these stocks have performed well recently (see the backtest results above), they could rebound again, provided there aren't too many negative catalysts.

RVER: Quality Looks Consistently Weak

I couldn't find any mention of quality on River1's website, so I'm disappointed but not surprised to see its profitability features are well below average. For example, the earlier table shows RVER's net margins about 9% lower than SPY (12.53% / 21.67%), and its ROE is 10% worse (18.38% / 28.03%). Notably, these margins are also lower than SPGP, GARP, and QARP, so I think what's happened here is that managers have traded quality for growth. This setup often works well in markets where sentiment is high, but it's hard to see it working in "both bull and bear markets," as is the fund's objective. RVER's stocks won't be attractive in a "flight to safety," and combined with its high portfolio beta, I can see it substantially underperforming if market sentiment turns negative.

Remember that RVER has high portfolio turnover, so these statistics are subject to change. However, I checked the sector-adjusted profit scores (derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades) in prior months, and it's generally been poor, as follows:

  • October 2024: 6.88/10
  • November 2024: 6.85/10
  • December 2024: 7.66/10
  • January 2025: 7.91/10
  • February 2025: 7.88/10
  • March 2025: 8.26/10
  • Current: 7.93/10

Investment Recommendation

I'm interested in tracking RVER because it offers tremendous growth potential at a price comparable to an S&P 500 Index fund. This finding is consistent with statements made on River1's website, and over the long run, it's possible it could outperform SPY and other GARP funds if the market conditions are right. However, I'm skeptical of the claim that it can outperform in both bull and bear markets. Selecting only 12-25 stocks is risky on its own, but RVER's current selections are extremely volatile, and they're relatively poor quality, suggesting they might struggle in market drawdowns. Therefore, while interesting, I don't recommend buying RVER now, but I will watch it each month and provide an update if its fundamentals improve. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Parnassus Value Select ETF Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

While U.S. equities broadly had a difficult quarter, value stocks posted a modest gain as measured by the Russell 1000 Value Index. After the new administration took office, post-election optimism regarding lower regulation and business-friendly policies gave way to the reality of restrictive fiscal policies and erratic tariff announcements creating uncertainty and recession fears. Volatility rose significantly, and investor sentiment shifted away from growth-oriented stocks—including many large technology names focused on semiconductors and/or artificial intelligence (AI) innovation— and toward stocks perceived as defensive. Against this risk-off backdrop, the best sector performers in the Russell 1000 Value Index were the Energy, Communication Services and Health Care sectors, while the Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary sectors performed the worst.

Performance data quoted represent past performance and are no guarantee of future returns. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted, and current performance information to the most recent month end is available on the Parnassus website (www.parnassus.com). Investment return and principal value will fluctuate, so an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original principal cost. Returns shown in the table do not reflect the deduction of taxes a shareholder may pay on fund distributions or redemption of shares. The Russell 1000 Value Index is an unmanaged index of common stocks, and it is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index figures do not take any expenses, fees or taxes into account, but mutual fund returns do. The estimated impact of

individual stocks on the Fund’s performance is provided by FactSet.

Quarterly Performance Review

Health Care holdings weighed on relative performance

The Fund returned -2.06% (net of fees) during the quarter, underperforming the Russell 1000 Value’s 2.14%. The Health Care sector was the largest detractor from relative performance due to stock selection, mitigated somewhat by a favorable overweight. Stock selection and an overweight in Information Technology had negative impacts. On the upside, stock selection in the Industrials and Financials sectors lifted relative results.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Baxter International (BAX) announced robust fourth-quarter financial results and projected full-year 2025 profit above Wall Street expectations, bolstering investor confidence in the stock. Medical device makers like Baxter are benefiting from increased demand for elective surgeries as the population ages.

Verizon Communications (VZ), which pays the highest dividend of any stock in the Dow Jones Industrial Index, drew investor interest in the quarter’s risk-off, defensively focused environment.

AstraZeneca’s (AZN)(OTCPK:AZNCF) shares rose after the company announced a favorable outcome from investigations by the Chinese government into allegations of improper drug importation, which investors had feared could result in much more costly fines.

Visa (V) reported quarterly earnings that topped consensus expectations and issued optimistic guidance at its latest Investor Day. In a market environment where investors turned to more defensive options, Visa benefited from being relatively resilient for a payments stock, given its domestic debit card exposure, which is primarily grocery-related.

Deere & Company (DE) stock gained as investors grew optimistic for a recovery in earnings amid signs of improvement in the sales cycle for agricultural equipment. Rising energy and commodity prices also buoyed the stock.

Bottom Quarterly Contributors

Bio-Rad Laboratories (BIO) shares fell during the quarter as investors reacted to multiple factors, including plans to reduce its workforce and declining sales in its Life Science division. A one-time issue around lower reimbursement from diabetes testing in China also pressured the stock.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) shares fell sharply in January following the debut of China-based DeepSeek’s AI model, which was reportedly much more inexpensively trained than other models. We remain optimistic that demand for AI semiconductors will grow despite algorithmic efficiencies.

Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) reported slower-than-expected growth for its cloud business, depressing sentiment on the stock. Concerns over capital spending on AI also played a role in the stock’s decline. Amazon.com’s outsize spending on AI initiatives caused some concern among investors, given its potential to dent profit margins. Macro concerns also weighed on the stock, including the prospect of worsened consumer sentiment leading to lower spending.

Agilent Technologies (A), a supplier of equipment and software for life sciences research, relies substantially on academic and government end markets, so recent cuts to grants from the National Institutes of Health impacted its near-term outlook.

Return calculations are gross of fees, time weighted and geometrically linked. Returns would be lower as a result of the deduction of fees.

Portfolio Positioning

Staying invested in quality franchises

Health Care, Information Technology and Communication Services are our top three sector overweights relative to the benchmark. Given a more concentrated portfolio, we generally expect the ETF to exhibit greater deviation from the benchmark during periods of high volatility. This was reflected in returns for the ETF this quarter. However, we believe the market sell-off also provides cleaner positioning for a rebound in quality stocks. We remain optimistic for companies we hold in the ETF to outperform over the long term.

The ETF's Health Care holdings include a mix of cyclical and defensive ideas. As the market sold off, our defensive Health Care names held their value and contributed to returns.

While the Health Care sector is currently experiencing regulatory uncertainty, we believe aging demographics and rising healthcare spending in developing countries make the long-term risk-reward attractive in the sector.

Our overweight in Information Technology is tied to cloud and AI adoption, where we continue to expect secular growth despite some near-term softness. The majority of our Communications Services exposure is tied to the telecommunication services industry, which helped the portfolio with downside protection during the quarter.

The ETF’s holdings remained relatively steady in the quarter. Only a few trades were made to recycle capital from early outperformers (such as Alphabet) to other names with more potential upside.

During this period of uncertainty, our focus is on opportunistically adding exposure to secular themes when we find bargains due to temporary reversals in investor sentiment. These areas of secular opportunity within the ETF include drug innovation, life science tools, managed care, AI, cloud, payments, e-commerce, agriculture mechanization and transport infrastructure.

Outlook

Focus on quality and valuation during this period of uncertainty

U.S. stocks suffered their worst first quarter since 2022 as sudden shifts in domestic regulatory priorities, government austerity and trade tensions widened the range of outcomes for the U.S. economy. After two consecutive years of double-digit returns, markets are now going through a period of adjustment as businesses and consumers evaluate an altered global macroeconomic landscape. The uncertain impact of tariffs on the prices of goods and services is also giving the Fed further pause on lowering interest rates. This is a notable change from market expectations at the time of U.S. elections last November. However, progress on the administration's priorities around tax cuts, deregulation and eventually lower rates could revive investor optimism.

We believe this heightened period of uncertainty should favor resilient businesses with strong market positions and create bargain opportunities that can reward long-term, patient investors. Roughly 90% of the Fund is invested in companies that have an economic moat, with roughly 60% invested in wide moat companies, as defined by Morningstar. These companies are likely to emerge stronger as they continue to invest to widen their competitive advantages.

Our fundamental bottom-up process involves ongoing assessment of downside risk and rotation of capital into companies that offer higher reward for lower risk. We focus on companies with durable competitive advantages and sustainable growth prospects, ensuring that our portfolio is well positioned to weather economic uncertainties. Our disciplined approach to investing in quality businesses at attractive valuations aims to deliver strong returns for our investors over the long term.

Glossary

Important Information

The Russell 1000® Value Index is a market capitalization weighted index that measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth rates. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2 year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years). The Russell 1000® Value Index is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased barometer for the large cap value segment. The index is completely reconstituted annually to ensure new and growing equities are included and that the represented companies continue to reflect value characteristics.

This agreement will not be terminated prior to May 1, 2026, and may be continued indefinitely by the investment adviser on a year-to-year basis.

ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL AND GOVERNANCE (ESG) GUIDELINES: The Fund evaluates ESG factors as part of the investment decision-making process, considering a range of impacts they may have on future revenues, expenses, assets, liabilities and overall risk. The Fund also utilizes active ownership to encourage more sustainable business policies and practices and greater ESG transparency. Active ownership strategies include proxy voting, dialogue with company management and sponsorship of shareholder resolutions, and public policy advocacy. There is no guarantee that the ESG strategy will be successful.

Investing involves risk and principal loss is possible. ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to NAV. Shares of any ETF are bought and sold at market prices (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns.
