data.title: TSMC Q1 Preview: Beating Trump's Tariffs
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) (OTC:TSMWF), (shorthand TSMC) are down roughly 21% since the last time I wrote on the key semiconductor company in January. At the time of my last piece of research, shares were worth just over $200. They've since dipped to the mid-$150s.

Despite this slight drop off, I remain super bullish on the firm. I believe that AI chip demand is still powering robust growth, which will only continue to push demand for their fab services higher. Looking at this from a bigger picture, shares have moved up 50% since the start of 2024. This is incredible growth and well ahead of the market. The company is still a compounder.

With this, TSMC needs to show investors how the future for them is Made in the USA. I touched on this before in some of my previous coverage from last year. TSMC needs to derisk their business from a Chinese invasion of Taiwan. U.S. plants are part of this diversification strategy. Now, however, these factories will likely become the heart of what gives this company a huge edge in the fab space.

In essence, their future is made in the USA.

While 2024 was a fruitful year for TSMC, I am still convinced that 2025 will be even stronger due to sustainable AI demand from some GPU designers and inference AI demand.

In my opinion, TSMC's fundamentals don't just still justify a strong buy rating, they also point to further upside. I believe the company is well-positioned heading into earnings Thursday.

Why I Am Doing Follow-Up Coverage

The main reason why I wanted follow-up coverage on TSMC is because recent developments point to the company fundamentally changing their supply chain and manufacturing strategy. To recap, at the tail end of 2024, the U.S. Commerce Department finalized a $6.6 billion government subsidy (plus up to $5 billion in low-cost government loans) for TSMC's production plant in Arizona. Most investors already knew about TSMC's play for CHIPS act funds. What is key (and what is changing) is their new focus on making US manufacturing the forefront of their future fabs (vs. an afterthought).

Recent announcements by both GPU giant NVIDIA (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) mean that some of the best future GPUs that power key data centers will now be built here in the US, thanks to TSMC putting their fabs in states like Arizona.

With this, the company is now experiencing some of the best fab yields globally from their plants in Arizona. This has huge implications for where the US sits in the global semiconductor supply chain. It also means that it's far more likely that US investors reward the company's shares with a higher P/E multiple. I believe earnings will be a huge catalyst.

TSMC: Made In America

In January, TSMC opened its Phoenix plant, and progress appears to be incredible. In the three months that the plant has been in operation, the 4nm (4-nanometer) line is already yielding about 4% more usable chips than the company's Taiwanese counterparts. Not only does this early success in the U.S. offset higher local operating costs, but it also validates the broader expansion strategy.

I also think it benefits TSMC that they have begun to produce chips on U.S. soil to offset geopolitical risks.

Driven by policies like the 2022 CHIPS Act, which is allocating over $52 billion for domestic semiconductor production, directly supports TSMC's expansion. TSMC's Arizona facility is set to benefit from the $6.6 billion in federal grants I mentioned before. This will help accelerate plans for advanced nodes like 5nm today and eventually 2nm by 2028.

Advanced manufacturing at their Arizona facilities is not new on its own. What is new is the fact that these facilities are now beating their advanced rival facilities in Taiwan.

Just in time as well, the Trump administration announced this past week new tariffs on semiconductors, which will likely come later this year as part of their trade strategy to make more critical manufacturing items here in the U.S.

To offset this, some of TSMC's major customers like Nvidia are also using TSMC's Phoenix fab for its up-and-coming Blackwell AI chips. AMD is also moving to build more chips in the US to avoid any new tariff risks.

In essence, made in the USA is not just a government incentive play by TSMC. It's a fundamentally different approach for them to navigate a trade environment that is becoming more difficult to work in. For TSMC, the optimal strategy is to make more stuff in the US. Customers are rewarding them for this investment. I expect this to play a greater role in their supply chain strategy going forward.

Q1 Preview

TSMC is expected to report its Q1 earnings on Thursday, and given where revenue figures came in for Q1, the expectation is that the company will post strong earnings. As of right now, four analysts project that TSMC will finish the quarter with an EPS of $2.07. If this EPS is met, it would represent a 49.64% year-over-year increase.

Additionally, 21 analysts predict TSMC to bring in $25.77 billion in revenue this quarter, which would be a 41.37% year-over-year increase. Already, Q1 revenue has been reported at $25.6 billion by Reuters. So we should expect the final Q1 figure that will be reported as a part of Q1 to come closely in line with this.

TSMC's incredible growth in Q1 is largely fueled by rampant AI chip demand, with January-March sales coming in strong and slightly beating consensus estimates. I expect their new U.S. fab to only increase demand from here. Demand could even shift from competitor Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), given their US fab is not expected to be operational until next year.

Valuation

While TSMC has some incredible catalysts, the world's largest Semiconductor fab only has a forward Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 17.38. When we compare this number to the sector median of 18.85, we see that this is actually a 7.78% discount. Seeking Alpha Quant gives TSMC a grade of a B- on this metric.

On this same front, TSMC has an incredibly attractive forward PEG ratio. Right now, TSMC's forward PEG ratio is at 0.58, which is a 58.26% discount to the sector median of 1.39. Seeking Alpha Quant gives TSMC a grade of an A on this metric. I really believe that the market is seriously underestimating Taiwan Semiconductor's forward growth.

With this, I think we should see TSMC shares trade at least at the sector median forward PEG ratio. If we saw this, this would represent 141.37% upside for the world's largest chip fab company. 141% may seem like a lot of upside. But to be clear, I think this is driven by investors appreciating the company's new made in the USA strategy and what that means for the company's operation stability.

Risks

I think the bull case for TSMC is super strong, but I will admit it's not risk-free.

I think the biggest risk is a potential AI GPU slowdown. I think there is some risk that some customers double-ordered or built up excessive inventories. If this happens, we could see Semi momentum halt later on in the second half of this year.

I think TSMC's biggest hedge for this is their US fabs. I believe we're likely to see tariffs on semis from this administration. TSMC's fabs in Arizona should have incredible pricing power going forward as a result of this. With this, on the operations side, I remain a little skeptical on the overall success of the Arizona fab ramp-up. As I mentioned earlier, the Phoenix fab has already seen incredible success, but it is still early. The fab has only been in operation for about three months, which means that there isn't a large sample size to pull from. Labor shortages (because this is specialty manufacturing) have plagued the US semi space in general. TSMC will likely have a supply side problem vs. a demand side.

Even then, I think it's important to recognize that roughly 90% of their production is still coming from Taiwan. In the long run, because there are a lot of tensions between China and Taiwan, they are going to have to spend a ton on Capex to help offset this risk. An invasion of Taiwan is more on the black-swan side of the risk bell curve. That doesn't mean it's impossible. My valuation estimates for the company leave the firm at the sector median forward PEG ratio. If we could see more of their production take place outside Taiwan, then I think their PEG ratio should be meaningfully higher than the sector median.

Bottom Line

While shares have fallen off over 20% YTD, I remain a strong buy on shares of TSMC.

While the AI boom is still one of the biggest trades on the market, the world's largest fab company is still riding incredible tailwinds driven by the AI boom, and their leadership in advanced nodes (particularly 3nm). Now, with the firm getting serious about U.S. production capacity, I think that the company is in an even greater spot. The company is really firing on all cylinders. There's a lot of room for shares to move up from here.

TSMC has proven that it is able to provide a consistent record of delivering strong earnings beats and robust top line growth. Top line growth is already evident for Q1, with a 42% revenue spike in Q1. Its growing U.S. footprint doesn't just enhance political support, but it also delivers critical wins - like Nvidia's decision to produce AI chips in America.

The company is also trading at very attractive multiples in comparison to its peers. I think the current valuation mismatch provides significant upside potential for investors willing to be in a Taiwan company that is becoming more American with every semiconductor it produces.

With this, shares remain a strong buy heading into the Q1 report.

data.title: J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: DiaMedica Therapeutics: Stroke Developer With Low Cash Reserve
data.description:
This is my first DiaMedica Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DMAC) article. DiaMedica is a clinical stage biopharmaceutical company with one primary product in clinical development for two indications. These are:

  1. DM199 in a Pivotal Phase 2/3 trial for the treatment of acute ischemic stroke [AIS] and
  2. DM199 in a Phase 2 trial for Preeclampsia.

In this article, I will reference DiaMedica's Q4, 2024 business update financial results press release, earnings conference call and 10-K, all released on 3/17/2025.

DiaMedica's lead DM199 indication has an intriguing profile.

General

In its 10-K business overview addressing its lead candidate DM199 (rinvecalinase alfa) therapy in AIS, DiaMedica characterizes it as:

  1. the first pharmaceutically active synthetic form of the human tissue kallikrein-1 (rhKLK1) protein to be clinically studied in patients;
  2. granted Fast Track Designation by FDA;
  3. kallikrein-1 (KLK1), extracted from human urine, is an established therapeutic modality in Asia for the treatment of AIS;
  4. being synthetic eliminates concerns about pathogen transmission.

An 01/2025 peer-reviewed publication described in the conference call provides scientific insight into:

...DM199's mechanism for increasing collateral circulation and salvaging brain tissue at risk from infarction following an AIS.

AIS

Aged and aging worthies such as myself who are aware of the F-A-S-T stroke recognition acronym may despair at ever getting treatment within the recommended time. Even if a person gets to the hospital on a timely basis, there are often delays in getting proper treatment.

The problem is that stroke treatments typically work best when administered soon after stroke onset. DM199 is working to change this, extending the time within which treatment may be effective from a few hours to up to 24 hours.

Slide 27 above from DiaMedica's 04/2025 Corporate Presentation shows how this dramatically improves the percentage of patients who can benefit from therapy. It posits that 90% of patients can reach the emergency department within 24 hours compared to 20% who can within 4.5 hours to qualify for tPA the only FDA approved therapy.

Details of trial

DiaMedica dubbed its phase 2/3 AIS trial ReMEDy2. It characterizes it as an:

...adaptive design, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial studying the use of the Company’s product candidate, DM199, to treat acute ischemic stroke (AIS) patients. Patients enrolled in the trial will be treated for three weeks with either DM199 or placebo, beginning within 24 hours of the onset of AIS symptoms, with the final follow-up at 90 days.

It dosed its first US patient in 04/2024. Its clinicaltrials.gov identifier is NCT05065216.

The study completion is listed as anticipated in 12/2026. A formal interim analysis will be conducted after 200 participants complete their Day 90 assessment. The purposes of this interim analysis are to assess safety, allow early stopping of the study for futility, or continue the study with a revised final sample up to a maximum of 728 participants.

During the earnings call, CMO Masuoka discussed the challenges DiaMedica was facing in achieving a critical mass of sites to generate a steady stream of enrollments.

Competition

The 10-K describes the competitive landscape. There is only 1 FDA approved therapy for AIS; it is tPA, brand name Activase; its therapeutic window is limited to up to 4.5 hours after onset of the AIS. Presentation slides 26 and 37 estimate the size of the AIS opportunity in the US as from $5 billion to >$10 billion.

Unsurprisingly, this enormous potential market has bred significant competition from pharmas of all sizes around the world. The 10-K lists 11 different pharmaceuticals currently in various stages of development. It seems quite likely that by the time DM199 is approved it will face an entirely different, more competitive, market than that prevailing in 04/2025.

DiaMedica maintains a low liquidity profile with modest cash burn and a short cash runway

During the earnings call, CFO Kellen reported that DiaMedica's cash and investments at the close of 12/31/2024 totaled ~$44.1 million. Its cash burn as reflected by its net cash used in operating activities for 2024 was ~$22.1 million, compared to ~$18.7 million for 2023.

The year-over-year increase in burn was driven by:

... the combination of our increased net loss and the advance of deposit funds to vendors supporting our ReMEDy2 trial during 2024. These were partially offset by changes in operating assets and liabilities during 2024, particularly the increase in accrued liabilities related to our ReMEDy2 trial and ongoing manufacturing development activities as of December 31, 2024.

When going back to review its 10-Q quarterly cash burns of $6.6 million for Q1, 2024, $6.4 million for Q2, 2024 and $9.4 million for Q3, 2024 it shows a net cash increase of $0.3 million for Q4, 2024.

Kellen indicated that management's anticipated cash runway as of 12/31/2024 ran into Q3, 2026. Its stock has had a rough several months since its halcyon trading above $6.00 late in 12/2024.

It traded above $6.00 for several periods from 12/2024 until as late as 3/18/2025. As I write on 04/15/2025 it trades at $3.59. It has only traded above $4.00 on a single day so far in April 2025. The lesson I take from this is that management needs to have things set up to make hay while the sun shines.

We are currently in a period of great uncertainty. The environment could hardly be worse for a little biotech like DiaMedica to raise cash. It would have been far better if management had been prepared to offer stock to the market before it needed to.

Limited near term catalysts make DiaMedica a high risk speculative "Hold"

DiaMedica's Presentation overview slide 4 below provides a snapshot of the company's key pipeline assets:

DiaMedica is working towards approval in two high-value indications. The four Wall Street Analysts who follow the stock have an average price target of $8.75 for an upside of ~156%. Therein lies the bull case.

I am more wary of the stock. It does not have any apparent market moving near term positive catalysts. Its cash runway means that it will soon need to raise additional funds, creating a significant near term negative catalyst.

Conclusion

I assess DiaMedica as having great potential over the mid to long term, if all goes well with its clinical trials. That is always a big if with clinical stage biotechs. Over the near term, DiaMedica is unattractive. In today's high-risk tariff infused market, it is not one that is worthwhile with an unproven biotech that has a cash runway that barely extends longer than a year.
